FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: approximately 1200 participants aged 9-13 years at baseline, 2-year follow-up assessment

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific smartphone/social media variables separately: TV watching (TVM), Video, Game (video games), Text (cell phone texting), SNS (social networking sites), VC (video chatting). Platform-specific screen use coefficients are separately extractable from analyses reported in Tables and Figures

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear mixed-effects (LME) models and Mendelian randomization analyses with inferential statistical tests. Reported causal effect coefficients for screen use predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes: TVM on picture vocabulary β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), TVM on withdrawn/depressed β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), VC on behavioral problems β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006)

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple β coefficients reported with p-values and credible intervals: β=-2.39 (corrected p<0.02), β=0.57 (corrected p<0.05), β=-0.30 (corrected p<0.0006), and numerous Mendelian randomization coefficients in Table 2 and supplementary materials with 95% credible intervals

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical longitudinal analysis using ABCD Study data with causal inference methods: LME models, genetic instrumental variables, Mendelian randomization (forward and reverse), and mediation analysis examining prospective associations over 2-year follow-up

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Advanced Science (2024), not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: FT-EC7 does not apply. Screen use/reading are independent variables (exposures) predicting cognitive and behavioral outcomes, not dependent variables. Paper reports both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses. Paper includes individual psychological/behavioral predictors (rule-breaking behavior, aggressive behavior, withdrawn/depressed symptoms, social problems, attention problems), which are not demographic-only predictors. Additionally, paper reports screen use→outcome associations and includes mediation/moderation analyses, all of which preclude FT-EC7 application
notes: Mendelian randomization approach with genetic instrumental variables provides causal inference evidence. Paper explicitly tests displacement hypothesis (screen use vs reading time competition) and mediation effects. Strong methodological rigor with forward/reverse MR analyses

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses U.S. ABCD Study data: N=5356 US adolescents, Year 2 (2018-2020, ages 11-12) and Year 4 (2020-2022, ages 13-14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures 'total recreational screen time' composite via ABCD Youth Screen Time Survey asking about watching television/videos, playing video games, texting, social media, and video chat. However, no smartphone-specific or social-media-specific coefficient is reported separately. Only aggregate composite 'total screen time' categorized as 0-4h, 4-8h, >8h daily is analyzed. Per FT-IC2 rule: 'Generic total screen time or STS composite with no phone/SM breakdown = NOT_MET'. E2 edge case: no phone/SM-specific row extractable from composite

FT_IC3.evidence: Paper fails at FT-IC2 criterion. Although Poisson and linear regression models report screen time associations (RR=1.09 for high vs low screen time with overweight/obesity), these statistics apply only to the aggregate composite screen time measure, not to any separately-measured smartphone or social media variable

FT_IC4.evidence: Although numeric statistics are reported (RR=1.09, 95% CI 1.02-1.16, p=0.013; B=3.27, 95% CI 1.54-4.99, p<0.001), these apply to the composite screen time exposure that fails FT-IC2. No extractable numerics for a qualifying smartphone/SM-specific variable

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical prospective cohort analysis using ABCD Study Years 2 and 4 with 2-year follow-up period; Poisson and linear regression models examining associations between baseline exposures and Year 4 outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Obesity (2026); original research article, not a preprint or duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper excluded at FT-IC2 (composite screen time without phone/SM breakdown). FT-EC7 assessment not applicable as paper does not meet inclusion criteria
notes: Paper measures Fitbit step count (objective) and self-reported screen time (composite). Although methodologically sound and prospective with strong sample size (N=5356), the failure to separately report a qualifying smartphone or social media variable coefficient is a critical exclusion criterion
